texts.shortText:
Medium-sized crab usually light cream or in life in colour, with a lighter patch on the gastric region that sometimes extend as a stripe running down the middle of the carapace (hard upper shell), occasionally with some mottling.

Carapace rounded, as long as wide, mostly smooth, though it is minutely pitted. Front of carapace upwardly curved with a depression in the middle down to the mouthparts. Chelipeds (arms) subequal and elongated, triangular (triquetral) in cross-section; surface finely granular, with a ridged upper margin; fingers as long as palms. Male chelipeds longer and broader than those of females.
Carapace length up to 20 mm, with chelipeds up to 42 mm long.

Global: Endemic to South Africa.

Local/Regional: Saldanha Bay to Algoa Bay, intertidal to 55 m depth.

Fairly unknown, though it is common on sandy substrates. Night active and walking over the sediment looking for prey. Able to quickly bury backwards into the sand, disappearing completely except for eyes and antenna sticking out. Extend into shallow lagoons such as Saldanha Bay as well as deep-water such as Agulhas Bank.

Not specifically known for this species, though many Leucosiidae crabs are scavengers. We have frequently observed this species walking over the soft sediments of False Bay at night, rocking from left to right moving the nippers of the extended chelipeds over the sediment in search for food that either lies on the ground or is slightly hidden or buried in the sand, probably feeding on small invertebrates such as worms, mollusks and crustaceans.

Not specifically known, but in similar species males transfer sperm to the female via the spermatophore, and eggs are fertilized as they are released and attached under the females tail until they hatch into planktonic larvae. Similar Leucosiidae crabs have shown ‘guarding’ behaviour, where males use their large chelipeds to guard females from other males. Thus, males with larger chelipeds usually have a reproductive advantage. It has been observed in the field that when males encounter pairing crabs, they successfully stole the females if the guardian was smaller than the challenger. Males and females have been observed to copulate with multiple partners, storing semen within the large seminal receptacles.

The only known species of round pebble crab with long legs in the Great African Seaforest and cannot be confused.

This is the only species in this endemic genus and is thought to be replaced farther east by Lyphira natalensis Galil, 2009 and Philyra samia Galil, 2009. Can easily be distinguished from these species by the triangular cross-sectioned arms.

Barnard, KH (1950). Descriptive Catalogue of South African Decapod Crustacea. Annals of the South African Museum, 38, 1–824. (p. 377)

Branch, G, Griffiths, CL, Branch, M & Beckley, L (2016). Two Oceans: A Guide to the Marine Life of Southern Africa (Fifth edition). Struik Nature, Cape Town. (p. 132).

Emmerson, WD (2016). A Guide to, and Checklist for, the Decapoda of Namibia, South Africa and Mozambique. Newcastle upon Tyne: Cambridge Scholars Publishing. Volumes 1–3, 526, 645, 711 pp.

Galil, BS (2009). An examination of the genus Philyra Leach, 1817 (Crustacea, Decapoda, Leucosiidae) with descriptions of seven new genera and six new species. Zoosystema, 31(2), 279-320.

Kobayashi, S & Archdale, MV (2020). Sexual traits and reproductive strategy of the leucosiid crab Pyrhila pisum. Journal of the Marine Biological Association of the United Kingdom, 100(6), 939-948.
